Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:

History of Present Illness:
Ms. ___ is a ___ F w PMHx of recent Alzheimer's
diagnosis who presents to ___ ED after being referred in by 
her
cognitive neurologist after examination was concerning for signs
and symptoms of ALS.
Per report, Ms. ___ has been experiencing a progressive
dysarthria, dysphagia, and fractured sleep for the last ___
months. Prior to that, there were some cognitive concerns and a
recent diagnosis of Alzheimer's dementia in ___. Since
then she has been living with her daughter - who is present at
the bedside to report the following history.
Ms. ___ current troubles began with shortness of breath
and trouble lying flat to sleep ~3 months ago. She complained of
pain in her chest and difficulty breathing when flat. Donepezil,
which had been started for AD, was entertained as a cause and
this was discontinued with no improvement in symptoms. Ms.
___ became progressively sleepier and sleepier during the
days, exacerbating her already present cognitive difficulties. 
In the last 2 months, she began to developed slurred speech and
dysphagia. At first her trouble was with thin liquids 
(aspiration
shown on a Barium swallow study) which has now progressed to
difficulty swallowing solids as well. She has been evaluated by
ENT and Pulmonology for her swallowing and respiratory concerns,
without definitive diagnosis. 
Her daughter also reports progressive weakness, more pronounced
in the upper extremities. Ms. ___ has difficulty with her
hands - opening jars and buttoning clothes. Her daughter also
reports that the family noticed fasciculations in the BUEs and
neck last ___ when Ms. ___ was wearing short sleeve
shirts.
Ms. ___ was evaluated in cognitive neurology clinic by Dr.
___ on the day of presentation. He was concerning by her
progressive picture, which seems most consistent with ALS.

 
Past Medical History:
- recent diagnosis of Alzheimer's type dementia
- recent sleep study: no REM sleep
- recent barium swallow: aspiration with thin liquids
- overactive bladder

___

NC
 
Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM:
VS T97.2 HR85 BP125/73 RR28 Sat95RA
GEN - elderly woman, head drooped, slurred speech
HEENT - NC/AT, MMM, no ptosis
NECK - weakness of neck flexors 4+/5, extensors strong
CV - RRR
RESP - moderately increased WOB with accessory muscle use, no
retractions, mild tachypnea to RR max of 31
ABD - soft, NT, ND
EXTR - atraumatic, WWP

NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION:
MS - Awakes to voice, but will fall asleep without verbal
stimulation. Oriented to self and date. Knows she is in a
hospital but cannot recall the name. Speech is extremely slurred
and difficult to understand, but there do not appear to be
language deficits. Further MS testing is compromised by
difficulty understanding speech. Voice is hypophonic and
enunciation of guttural sounds is quite compromised. No evidence
of apraxia or neglect.

CN - VFF to confrontation. PERRL. EOMI. No ptosis. No reported
diplopia. Facial sensation intact to LT. Face is symmetric at
rest and with activation - though there does appear to be
bilateral bulbar muscle weakness. Hearing intact to voice. 
Palate
with minimal excursion. Hypophonia with relatively more
compromised guttural sounds (haha, gaga). SCMs and traps full
power. Tongue with global atrophy.

MOTOR - Normal bulk. +Fasciculations in the BUEs (delts) and 
neck
muscles. BUEs with deltoid (___), tricep (4+/5) weakness. L ECR
and FEx are 4+/5. BLEs appear grossly full strength, with the
exception of 4+/5 at bilateral IPs.

SENSORY - Grossly intact to LT and temperature throughout.

REFLEXES - Markedly hyperreflexive throughout. 3+ at BUEs and
patellars. +Pec jerks bilaterally. Toes are down going
bilaterally.

COORD - No gross evidence of truncal or appendicular ataxia.

GAIT - Rises unassisted, steadier than expected. Able to take 
few
steps unaided.

 
Pertinent Results:
___ CT Head
1. No acute intracranial process. 
2. Moderate to severe age related involutional changes and 
moderate small 
vessel ischemic changes. 

___ CXR
Low lung volumes accentuate the bronchovascular markings.  
Elevated left 
hemidiaphragm with gas distended stomach and bowel beneath.  
Bibasilar 
atelectasis. 

___ EMG
Abnormal study. Together with the clinical presentation, the 
electrophysiologic findings are most consistent with a 
generalized disorder of motor neurons or their axons, as can be 
seen in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. However, chronic and 
ongoing left cervical and lumbosacral polyradiculopathies cannot 
be entirely excluded. 
There is incidental evidence for a median neuropathy at the left 
wrist, as in carpal tunnel syndrome. 
Results of repetitive nerve stimulation and short exercise 
testing point away from a disorder of neuromuscular transmission 
such as myasthenia ___.  
 
Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to the neurology service for workup of 
neuromuscular disorder as above.

She was admitted to the stepdown unit, however in the morning 
appeared sleepy, with head drop, tachypnea and ABG showing 
hypercarbia to the ___ and hypoxia to the ___. She was 
transferred to the ICU for BiPAP.

ICU COURSE:
No EMG was available on the weekend, and so while in the ICU, 
tensilon test was performed to look for signs of myasthenia 
___. This was positive - neck flexion improved as did eye 
closure strength, although speech remained hypophonic. She was 
then started on mestinon and was uptitrated. A pheresis line was 
placed and the patient received 2 sessions of PLEX. She was 
intermittently on and off BiPAP, with repeated ABGs that showed 
continued hypoxia and hypercarbia. She started developing skin 
breakdown on her nose from the BiPAP mask, which was treated 
with wound care.

EMG was performed (see results section), which established a 
diagnosis of ALS. The tensilon test can be positive in the 
setting of ALS and certain other neurological disorders. 
Mestinon and plasma exchange were discontinued. After discussion 
with the family, the patient was made DNR/DNI.

The patient was acutely worse on the morning of ___, with 
somnolence that did not improve with BiPAP. After further 
discussion with the family and palliative care consult, the 
decision was made to focus on comfort care. She was transferred 
out of the ICU.

In reviewing her outside MRI, her diagnosis of dementia is more 
likely frontotemporal dementia, which can be associated with 
ALS, rather than Alzheimer's dementia.

___ passed away on ___ at 0143AM. She had absent
pupillary responses, no pulses, and she did not have any heart 
or
breath sounds. Her daughter was at the bedside, and she elected
not to pursue an autopsy. The PCP was notified, and the death
certificate was signed.

The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete.
1. trospium 20 mg oral BID

___ yo man with history of normal pressure hydrocephalus, 
incontinence, gait disorder admitted after syncopal episode at 
home.  Per the patient's wife who was present at the time of the 
incident (the wife is home but contributes to the HPI via 
telephone with the daughter translating at the bedside), the 
patient was somewhat confused, but not more than normal, and 
rose from a seated position to stand up to go to bed last night 
when he lost consciousness.  He fell backwards, back into his 
chair and was said to have lost consciousness.  There was no 
incontinence or seizure like activity noted.  His wife activated 
EMS.  Per the daughter and wife, it's not clear when the patient 
awoke/regained consciousness.  There is no EMS report in the 
chart.  When the patient arrived at the ED, he was said to be 
awake and his VS 97.7, HR 89  BP 135/98  RR 18, 93% on RA.  
Patient was confused and at times agitated as he did not know 
where he was or why he was here.  EKG was without ischemic 
changes or evidence of arrhythmia.  CXR was limited by kyphosis 
but without infiltrate or acute process.  Head CT was unchanged 
from past.  He was given 1 liter of NS and admitted to Medicine 
for further evaluation.

On arrival to the floor, the patient denies any complaints, says 
he feels fine.  He specifically denies any chest pain, dyspnea, 
nausea, vomiting.  Of note, the patient is a seemingly 
unreliable reporter and disagrees with some of what his wife 
endorses in the ROS.  For instance, she says he occasionally 
gets Chest pain but the daughter who is speaking with the wife 
on the telephone discounts her mother's input.  ? reliability of 
the patient's wife input.

Conclusions  
The left atrium is mildly dilated. Left ventricular wall 
thickness, cavity size, and systolic function are normal 
(LVEF>55%). Due to suboptimal technical quality, a focal wall 
motion abnormality cannot be fully excluded. The estimated 
cardiac index is normal (>=2.5L/min/m2). Tissue Doppler imaging 
suggests a normal left ventricular filling pressure 
(PCWP<12mmHg). There is no left ventricular outflow obstruction 
at rest. Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are 
normal. The aortic valve leaflets are mildly thickened. No 
aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve appears 
structurally normal with trivial mitral regurgitation. There is 
an anterior space which most likely represents a fat pad. 
IMPRESSION: Normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved 
global biventricular systolic function. No structural cardiac 
cause of syncope identified.
 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
___ yo man with syncope at home that occurred upon rising from a 
seated position giving rise to the question that this may very 
well be related to orthostasis.  The patient fell backwards into 
his chair yet did not apparently regain consciousness for some 
period of time.  There was no evidence for seizure activity from 
history.  There is no evidence for arrhythmia on EKG or 
telemetry thus far, but an isolated arrhythmia is a possibility. 
 Given the patient's history of DVT and recurrence in the 
setting of therapeutic warfarin makes one concerned re: 
recurrent DVT and pulmonary embolism, however, there is not much 
else arguing for a PE - he is not tachycardic, he has no 
complaints of dyspnea and his O2 sat is 98% on RA.  
.
1) SYNCOPE: Monitored on telemetry x 48 hours.  EKG without 
acute ischemic changes, TTE as noted. No recurrences throughout 
hospitalization.

repeat tap would be useful and we consulted Neurology for their

Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. ___ is a ___ y/o female with dementia, CAD, h/o CVA, 
HLD, and HTN who presents to the hospital with unwitnessed fall 
found to have small scalp laceration but without acute 
intracranial hemorrhage and left elbow laceration. Also found to 
have evidence of UTI and a decreasing Hct.  

Active Issues: 
# Unwitnessed fall: Possible etiologies included syncope, 
presyncope, vasovagal as patient was in bathroom, hypovolemia in 
setting of infection, orthostasis, functional cause due to 
imbalance, cardiac cause. Patient with negative troponin and CK 
x 1 and EKG with some ST depression in anterior and lateral 
leads (likely evidence of previous ischemia), appeared well 
hydrated after fluids in the ED. Fall most likely secondary to 
poor balance and possible hypovolemia and delerium in setting of 
infection with UTI (see below). The patient was placed on fall 
precautions. She was monitored on telemetry for 24 hours and was 
in normal sinus rhythm without any events. Othostatic vital 
signs showed border line positivity, however patient was 
asymptomatic. She had no episodes of fall while in the hospital. 
She was evaluated by physical therapy who recommended for 
physical therapy rehab to gain strength and work on balance.  

# E. Coli UTI: Patient denies dysuria and frequency, however 
patient has dementia. UA showed large leukocytes, 182 WBCs, and 
many bacteria. Patient also with leukocytosis to 20k while in 
hospital. Urine culture showed >100,000 colonies of E. Coli 
sensitive to Bactrim. She was started on Bactrim DS and recieved 
3 days of treatment. She remained afebrile and leukocytosis 
trended down to 6k.

# Normocytic Anemia: The patient had a drop in hct from 33 to 23 
and then to 21. This is likely due to blood loss with injuries 
secondary to fall. The patient was transfused 2 units of PRBCs 
and her Hct bumped appropriately to 30. Given the patient has no 
other values in system since ___, it is unclear whether or not 
the 33 on addmission has other underlying cause. Other causes of 
microcytic anemia should be worked up as an outpatient. She 
remained without symptoms or signs of acute blood loss 
throughout addmission.

# Hypotension: The patient had a SBP ranging in the ___ - 100s 
while in hospital. Her BP medications were frequently held 
secondary to this. The cause could be secondary to the blood 
loss and improved with transfusion. Lasix was stopped and BP 
also improved to 100s-120s. This will need to be followed up on 
as an outpatient for possible adjustment of blood pressure 
medications.

Chronic Issues:
# CAD, native vessel: The patient is s/p 3 stents placed ___. 
Plavix, BB, statin, ASA were continued in the hospital. 
Metoprolol was held at times for low blood pressure.

# HTN, benging: Imdur and Lasix were held at times for low BP. 
lasix was stopped.

# HLD: Home crestor was continued.

# Dementia: Exelon PO was given in hospital as patch not 
available. The patient will transition back to patch as an 
outpatient.

Transitional Issues:
- The patient will likely need a work up of microcytic anemia as 
an outpatient.
- The patient may need adjustment of hypertension medications as 
an outpatient given low BP in hospital. Lasix was stopped while 
hospitalized.
-CXR on addmissiong showed: Calcified rounded opacity overlying 
the left lung base may be a chondral calcification.  A follow up

Brief Hospital Course:
___ NH resident w/ hx of recurrent falls and recurrent UTI 
present with new fall, presenting with subdural hematoma and 
UTI.
. 
ACTIVE ISSUES

# Subdural hematoma: Pt presented from OSH with subdural 
hematoma.  She was evaluated by neurosurgery in the ED and was 
thought to have acute on chronic SDH.  No intervention was 
indicated.  Neuro exam was nonfocal.  Repeated CT on following 
days did not reveal interval changes.  She was started on 
dilantin 1 g in the ED, and continued with 100 mg dilantin tid 
for 10 days while in the hospital.  This was discontinued prior 
to discharge.  
.
# UTI: Pt was incontinent and wore diaper at baseline.  UTI 
appears recurrent per discussion with covering physician at 
___.  No evidence of sepsis or pyelonephritis.  Pt 
was initially covered with vancomycin/cefepime for UTI, which 
was later converted to Zosyn to more completely covered a 
suspected aspiration pneumonia (see next issue) when urine 
culture was positive for pan-sensitive enterococcus. She 
completed a full course of 7 days while in house.

# Aspiration pneumonia with concurrent pulmonary edema: Although 
pt was afebrile and had no cough, she was initially treated with 
Vancomycin and Zosyn given the concerning chest X-ray findings 
in the ED.  She was evaluated by the speech and swallow team and 
they witness frank aspiration.  Her CXR revealed ?infiltrate in 
the right lower lobe along with mild pulmonary edema.  She was 
started on Zosyn for an 10-day course.  Her pulmonary edema was 
managed with Lasix and progress was tracked with CXRs that 
showed improvement.  An echocardiogram was done and showed no 
abnormalities.
 
# Dehydration with hypernatremia: On initial presentation, we 
appeared dry likely secondary to poor po intake in the setting 
of UTI.  Her ECHO last year was notable for pulmonary 
hypertension with moderate TR, but preserved LV function per OSH 
report.  She received NS boluses in the MICU, and tolerated well 
these well.  However, she developed pulmonary edema as above, 
managed with intermittent Lasix dosing.  An echocardiogram did 
not show any abnormalities, not even the ones indicated above 
(i.e. pulmonary hypertension, moderate TR).  Therefore, she was 
discharged without any standing Lasix due to concern for her 
poor PO intake.  Her sodium was managed initially with D5W, but 
this contributed to her volume overloaded state and we instead 
allowed her to drink to thirst to correct her sodium, which was 
normal on discharge.

# s/p fall: Pt had a history of recurrent fall per report from 
nursing home.  The mechanism of all has been felt to be 
mechanical.  EKG on this admission has been stable, and no 
cardiac enzyme elevation was observed.  Pt denied history of 
seizure.  Her TSH was normal.  She will require 1:1 assistance 
with all transfers and has been continually encouraged to ask 
for help getting out of bed.  
.

# Hyperkalemia with diarrhea: Pt takes kayaxelate on ___ and 
___ at home. With her worsening diarrhea in house, we stopped 
this and it should not be continued unless follow-up show 
worsening hyperkalemia.  Her C. diff toxin tests were negative 
x2.
.
# Depression: We continued her citalopram 20 mg daily and 
initially held mirtazepine to prevent sedation.  She should 
continue on both upon discharge.
.

creatinine
- Please encourage PO intake, keeping mind aspiration 
precautions
- Monitor oxygen saturation and please keep above 92% with nasal

INSTRUCTIONS AFTER ORTHOPAEDIC SURGERY:

- You were in the hospital for orthopedic surgery. It is normal 
to feel tired or "washed out" after surgery, and this feeling 
should improve over the first few days to week.

your weight bearing precautions strictly at all times.

MEDICATIONS:
- Please take all medications as prescribed by your physicians 
at discharge.
- Continue all home medications unless specifically instructed 
to stop by your surgeon.
- Do not drink alcohol, drive a motor vehicle, or operate 
machinery while taking narcotic pain relievers.
- Narcotic pain relievers can cause constipation, so you should 
drink eight 8oz glasses of water daily and take a stool softener 
(colace) to prevent this side effect.

ANTICOAGULATION:
- Please take Lovenox daily for 4 weeks

WOUND CARE:
- You may shower. No baths or swimming for at least 4 weeks.
- Any stitches or staples that need to be removed will be taken 
out at your 2-week follow up appointment.
- Please remain in your dressing and do not change unless it is 
visibly soaked or falling off.

Please call your PCP or surgeon's office and/or return to the 
emergency department if you experience any of the following:
- Increasing pain that is not controlled with pain medications
- Increasing redness, swelling, drainage, or other concerning 
changes in your incision
- Persistent or increasing numbness, tingling, or loss of 
sensation
- Fever > 101.4
- Shaking chills
- Chest pain
- Shortness of breath

medications down
- Any other medical concerns

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ___,

It was a pleasure caring for you at ___ 
___.  You were admitted to the hospital after a 
fall and were found to have a right upper arm fracture, two left 
rib fractures and a urinary tract infection.  The Orthopaedic 
Service evaluated your arm fracture and no surgery was 
necessary.  Please remain non-weight bearing on your right arm 
and continue to wear your sling for comfort.  Your rib fractures 
will heal on their own, but it is important to use your 
incentive spirometer to help keep your lungs inflated to prevent 
pneumonia.  You were started on an oral antibiotic to treat your 
urinary tract infection. 

You have worked with Physical Therapy and you are now ready to 
be discharged from the hospital.  Please follow the discharge 
instructions below to ensure a safe recovery:

Instructions for Rib Fractures: 

* Your injury caused left-sided rib fractures which can cause 
severe pain and subsequently cause you to take shallow breaths 
because of the pain.
 
* You should take your pain medication as directed to stay ahead 
of the pain otherwise you won't be able to take deep breaths. If 
the pain medication is too sedating take half the dose and 
notify your physician.
 
* Pneumonia is a complication of rib fractures.  In order to 
decrease your risk you must use your incentive spirometer 4 
times every hour while awake.  This will help expand the small 
airways in your lungs and assist in coughing up secretions that 
pool in the lungs.
 
* You will be more comfortable if you use a cough pillow to hold 
against your chest and guard your rib cage while coughing and 
deep breathing.
 
* Symptomatic relief with ice packs or heating pads for short 
periods may ease the pain.
 
* Narcotic pain medication can cause constipation therefore you 
should take a stool softener twice daily and increase your fluid 
and fiber intake if possible.
 
* Do NOT smoke
 
* If your doctor allows, non-steroidal ___ drugs 
are very effective in controlling pain ( ie, Ibuprofen, Motrin, 
Advil, Aleve, Naprosyn) but they have their own set of side 
effects so make sure your doctor approves.
 
* Return to the Emergency Room right away for any acute 
shortness of breath, increased pain or crackling sensation 
around your ribs (crepitus).
 
General Trauma Surgery Instructions:

Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the 
Emergency Department for any of the following:
*You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or 
tightness.
*New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze.
*If you are vomiting and cannot keep down fluids or your 
medications.
*You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea, 
or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid 
heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.
*You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a 
bowel movement.
*You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your 
urine, or experience a discharge.
*Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone 
within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is 
getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or 
back.
*You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees 
Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius.
*Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern 
you.

Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically 
advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take 
any new medications as prescribed.

Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times 
per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. Avoid lifting 
weights greater than ___ lbs until you follow-up with your 
surgeon.
Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain 
medications.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient with history of chronic recurrent cholangitis was 
admitted to the General Surgical Service for elective 
cholecystectomy. On ___, the patient underwent open 
cholecystectomy, which went well without complication (reader 
referred to the Operative Note for details). After a brief, 
uneventful stay in the PACU, the patient arrived on the floor 
NPO, on IV fluids, with a foley catheter and a JP drain in 
place, and epidural for pain control. The patient was 
hemodynamically stable.
Post-operative pain was initially well controlled with epidural 
analgesia, which was converted to oral pain medication when 
tolerating clear liquids. The patient was started on sips of 
clears on POD# 1. Diet was progressively advanced as tolerated 
to a regular diet by POD# 2. The epidural and foley catheter was 
discontinued at midnight of POD# 4. The patient subsequently 
voided without problem. JP was discontinued on POD# 4 as the 
output was low. On POD 6, patient was noticed to have drainage 
from lateral aspect of incision. Dressing was placed over the 
incision. On POD 7, drainage increased, patient's labs were 
noticeable for WBC of 18K. Patient's wound was opened and 
cultured. Dry packing was placed into the wound after wound was 
irrigated, patient was started on Zosyn. On POD 8, patient's WBC 
was 27.6, patient was pan cultured, she underwent liver US, 
which demonstrated post operative changes and was negative for 
fluid collection. Urine cultures were positive for E.coli. On 
POD 10, patient developed loose stool, her C. diff was negative. 
She underwent Chest CT secondary to increased SOB on exertion. 
CT demonstrated small pulmonary effusions and atelectasis, 
patient was given 20 of Lasix IV to diuresis. On the following 
days, patient's WBC started to downward, she remained afebrile. 
ID was consulted for antibiotics recommendations. They 
recommended that the patient be on zosyn for a 2 week period, 
beginning from ___. For this reason, a picc line was placed in 
the patient. A c. diff was sent on this patient which was 
negative. On post op day ___, a vac was placed on the 
patients wound and there was no leak. This vac will need to be 
changed every 3 days. On post op day 14 the patient was 
discharged to rehab. 

During admission, patient was follow by Geriatric team for 
managing of acute delirium and insomnia. Geriatric 
recommendations were followed, patient's mental status returned 
to baseline, and insomnia improved. Patient was evaluated by 
physical therapy and was discharged in rehabilitation to 
continue recovery. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating with assist, voiding without assistance, and 
pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge 
teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding 
verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.

Brief Hospital Course:
MICU COURSE
===========
Mr. ___ is a ___ man with history of AAA, CAD, 
stroke, status post fall x2 recently, with L1 bony Chance 
fracture s/p operative repair with discharged to rehab, 
currently living at rehab, who presented to ___ with 
lethargy and altered mental status, transferred to ___ with 
AAA grown to 7.9
x 7.4cm, ~1x1" past month, c/f leaking, and hypotension. 
Etiology felt to be ___ urosepsis vs hypovolemia (poor PO 
intake). He was initially treated for urosepsis with abx, which 
were peeled off when cultures returned negative. He received 
significant fluid resuscitation leading to improvement in his 
mental status and hemodynamics improved. His AAA was evaluated 
by vascular surgery and felt to not be contributing to his 
hypotension. He was transferred to the vascular surgery service 
for operative management of his AAA. 

MEDICINE FLOOR COURSE
======================
Patient was found to be C. diff positive (PCR and toxin assay) 
and was continued on PO vancomycin. Urine cultures from ___ 
growing E. coli and Pseudomonas, so patient was continued on 
ceftazidime. TTE showed no evidence of heart failure, thoug 
patient was grossly anasarcic. Remained mildly hypotensive 
though asymptomatic while on medicine floor. 

CVICU COURSE
===========
Patient was taken to the OR for an EVAR. The groins were closed 
via perclose b/l. There were no complications and the patient 
tolerated the procedure well. For details of the operation see 
the surgeon's operative report. The patient received 2platelets, 
2ffp, 3prbc and his postop Hct 26.3. The patient remained 
intubated and was taken to the CVICU for recovery. His CVICU 
course was uncomplicated. He was weaned to extubation. He was 
weaned off pressor support as well. He failed a bedside swallow 
and a DHT was placed for enteral access. Given his dramatically 
fluid overloaded state, the patient was started on a Lasix drip 
to aid in equilibration of his fluid status. Given his 
appropriate recovery, he was transferred to the VICU.

VICU COURSE
============
The patient continued to recover well in the VICU. His DHT tube 
feeds were advanced to goal without issue. His Lasix drip was 
exchanged for schedule daily torsemide. Unfortunately, the 
patient became very resistant to therapy, including ___ and SLP. 
He declined sessions, declined medications, and decline many of 
his routine cares from nursing. Ultimately, he even removed his 
DHT. In the setting of these barriers, many goals of care 
discussions were had with the family and the patient. 
Ultimately, the patient agreed to work with SLP and his diet was 
advanced with 1:1 supervision. Given his inability to take 
adequate PO initially, he was supported with TPN. Patient 
refused medications and refused PO intake, ethics was consulted 
and multiple meetings with ethics team, SW,CM, family members, 
Mr. ___ and the team were had. Patient reported on ___ he 
would like to go home with hospice. DNR/DRI status was discussed 
with family and patient. On ___ arrangements were made and 
patient was discharged home. Follow up appointment were 
scheduled and discharge instructions were discussed with patient 
and family.

Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you at ___ 
___.  You were admitted to the hospital after 
placement of a stent graft in your aorta to strengthen the part 
of the artery that was weakened by an aneurysm.  To perform this 
procedure, small punctures were made in the arteries on both 
sides of your groin.  You tolerated the procedure well and are 
now ready to be discharged from the hospital.   Please follow 
the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and uneventful 
recovery.

Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Repair Discharge 
Instructions

PLEASE NOTE: After endovascular aortic repair (EVAR), it is very 
important to have regular appointments (every ___ months) for 
the rest of your life.  These appointments will include a CT 
(聯CAT聰) scan and/or ultrasound of your graft.  If you miss an 
appointment, please call to reschedule. 
WHAT TO EXPECT:
聲Bruising, tenderness, and a sensation of fullness at the groin 
puncture sites (or incisions) is normal and will go away in 
one-two weeks
CARE OF THE GROIN PUNCTURE SITES:
聲It is normal to have mild swelling, a small bruise, or small 
amounts of drainage at the groin puncture sites.  In two weeks, 
you may feel a small, painless, pea sized knot at the puncture 
sites.  This too is normal.  Male patients may notice swelling 
in the scrotum.  The swelling will get better over one-two 
weeks.
聲Look at the area daily to see if there are any changes.  Be 
sure to report signs of infection.  These include: increasing 
redness; worsening pain; new or increasing drainage, or drainage 
that is white, yellow, or green; or fever of 101.5 or more.  (If 
you have taken aspirin, Tylenol, or other fever reducing 
medicine, wait at least ___ hours after taking it before you 
check your temperature in order to get an accurate reading.)
FOR SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site or 
incision)
聲If you have sudden, severe bleeding or swelling at either of 
the groin puncture sites: 
-Lie down, keep leg straight and apply (or have someone apply) 
firm pressure to area for ___ minutes with a gauze pad or 
clean cloth. 
-Once bleeding has stopped, call your surgeon to report what 
happened.   
-If bleeding does not stop, call ___ for transfer to closest 
Emergency Room. 
聲You may shower 48 hours after surgery.  Let the soapy water 
run over the puncture sites, then rinse and pat dry.  Do not rub 
these sites and do not apply cream, lotion, ointment or powder. 
聲Wear loose-fitting pants and clothing as this will be less 
irritating to the groin puncture sites. 
MEDICATIONS
聲Take aspirin daily.  Aspirin helps prevent blood clots that 
could form in your repaired artery. 
聲It is very important that you never stop taking aspirin or 
other blood thinning medicines-even for a short while- unless 
the surgeon who repaired your aneurysm tells you it is okay to 
stop.   Do not stop taking them, even if another doctor or nurse 
tells you to, without getting an okay from the surgeon who first 
prescribed them. 
聲You will be given prescriptions for any new medication started 
during your hospital stay.
聲Before you go home, your nurse ___ give you information about 
new medication and will review all the medications you should 
take at home.  Be sure to ask any questions you may have.  If 
something you normally take or may take is not on the list you 
receive from the nurse, please ask if it is okay to take it. 
PAIN MANAGEMENT
聲Most patients do not have much pain following this procedure.  
 Your puncture sites may be a little sore.  This will improve 
daily. If it is getting worse, please let us know.
聲You will be given instructions about taking pain medicine if 
you need it.
ACTIVITY
聲You must limit activity to protect the puncture sites in your 
groin.  For ONE WEEK:
-Do not drive
-Do not swim, take a tub bath or go in a Jacuzzi or hot tub
-Do not lift, push, pull or carry anything heavier than five 
pounds
-Do not do any exercise or activity that causes you to hold your 
breath or bear down with your abdominal muscles.
-Do not resume sexual activity
聲Discuss with your surgeon when you may return to other regular 
activities, including work.  If needed, we will give you a 
letter for your workplace. 
聲It is normal to feel weak and tired.  This can last six-eight 
weeks, but should get better day by day.   You may want to have 
help around the house during this time.
___ push yourself too hard during your recovery.  Rest when 
you feel tired.  Gradually return to normal activities over the 
next month.
聲We encourage you to walk regularly. Walking, especially 
outdoors in good weather is the best exercise for circulation.  
Walk short distances at first, even in the house, then do a 
little more each day.
聲It is okay to climb stairs.  You may need to climb them slowly 
and pause after every few steps. 

DIET
聲It is normal to have a decreased appetite.  Your appetite will 
return over time.
聲Follow a well balance, heart-healthy diet, with moderate 
restriction of salt and fat. 
聲Eat small, frequent meals with nutritious food options (high 
fiber, lean meats, fruits, and vegetables) to maintain your 
strength and to help with wound healing.
BOWEL AND BLADDER FUNCTION
聲You should be able to pass urine without difficulty.  Call you 
doctor if you have any problems urinating, such as burning, 
pain, bleeding, going too often, or having trouble urinating or 
starting the flow of urine.   Call if you have a decrease in the 
amount of urine. 
聲You may experience some constipation after surgery because of 
pain medicine and changes in activity.  Increasing fluids and 
fiber in your diet and staying active can help.  To relief 
constipation, you may talk a mild laxative.   Please take to 
your pharmacist for advice about what to take. 
SMOKING
聲If you smoke, it is very important that you STOP.   Research 
shows smoking makes vascular disease worse.  This could increase 
the chance of a blockage in your new graft.  Talk to your 
primary care physician about ways to quit smoking.

#Aspiration Pneumonia 
Presented with fall from his memory care unit had a full trauma 
survey in the emergency room which included a CT torso.  CT head 
and CT C-spine were negative for any trauma.  CT torso showed 
right lower lobe pneumonia and he was started on Unasyn and 
azithromycin.  He was transitioned to Augmentin and completed a

- You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
- You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
- You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
- All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
- Your incision may be slightly red around the edges. This is 
normal.
- If you have steri strips, do not remove them for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that are on your incision.) But 
if they fall off before that that's okay).
- You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
- It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This 
will go away.
- Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
- Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
- You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
- You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
- Over the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluitds and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
-You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine 
as directed.
- Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take 
more medicine at one time than prescribed.
- Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
- Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
- If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
- Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon.

wtih the orthopedic service who upon evaluation determined that 
no surgical intervention was needed. Incentive spirometry was 
encouraged and the patient's oxygen saturation was closely 
monitored.  The patient was placed on WBAT to bilateral lower 
extremities and physical therapy was ordered. After review of 
the c-spine, the patient was noted to have a right C7 
non-displaced transverse process fracture. A soft collar was 
applied to his neck for support.  After the patient's vital 
signs stabilized, he was transferred to the surgical floor on HD 
#3,

urinary tract infection and was started on a 3 day course of

withdrawal.  The valium dose was re-adjusted and discontinued on

began experiencing runs of rapid heart rate, later determined to 
be paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and he was subsequently 
started on intravenous metoprolol.  Upon discharge, his 
intraveous dose of metoprolol was converted to oral use and his 
heart rate normalized.  His electrolytes were monitored and 
cardiac enzymes were sent.  His cardiac enzymes were normal.

and altered mental status at her nursing home found to have pan 
sensitive E. Coli septicemia likely from a biliary source. 

# E. Coli Septicemia likely secondary to Cholangitis: Patient 
noted on initial blood cultures to have pan-sensitive E coli. 
She was initially started on Unasyn to cover likely biliary 
source, transitioned to ceftriaxone and then PO cefpodoxime with 
plan for total 14 day course (on day 5 of 14 on day of 
discharge). Likely biliary source of bacteria; initial concern 
for urinary source given GNR in urine, but later grew out 
pan-sensitive Klebsiella in urine. Most likely source of 
septicemia is cholangitis given initial intrahepatic ductal 
dilitation and CBD dilitation on CT; resolved by day prior to 
discharge per RUQ ultrasound. No evidence of stones in the 
gallbladder on day prior to discharge; no evidence of 
obstructive mass on imaging, and given the resolution of the 
symptoms, she likely had a transient obstruction from a stone 
that has since passed. The patient improved clinically and her 
LFTs and white count trended down, with WBC 12 on day of 
discharge.
.
# Pancreatitis: Patient with abdominal pain, elevated lipase, 
and dusky stranding around pancreas on CT scan on admission. 
Bisap score was 3 given age, SIRS criteria (fever and 
leukocytosis), and pleural effusion.  She was mildly 
hemoconcentrated with elevated hct and dry on exam.  Most likely 
cause of pancreatitis is gallstones given prominent CBD 
(although size normal for age) and intrahepatic ductal 
dilatation, though CBD diameter stable from previous imaging and 
resolved on RUQ u/s on day prior to discharge. No clear inciting 
medications, normal ___, and no EtOH. No evidence of malignancy 
causing obstruction based on CT scan, and symptoms resolved 
without intervention making persistant obstruction unlikely.  
Considered ERCP but felt risk outweighed potential benefit given 
rapid improvement with antibiotics and conservative management.  
She was initially managed conservatively with NPO, IVF, and diet 
was advanced which she tolerated. On day of discharge, abdominal 
pain resolved and all labs trending towards normal.
.
# HTN: The patient's SBP was quite labile, ranging from 100 on 
admission to 190 on the floor. Had been on metoprolol succinate 
12.5 mg daily which was held in the ICU given septicemia. 
Restarted on metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily on discharge. 
.
# Bacteruria: Patient's urine grew pan-sensitive Klebsiella. No 
urinary symptoms. Likely asymptomatic bacturia, not source of 
septicemia; covered by cefpodoxime.
.

obstruction, strangulation, or incarceration. Initially elevated 
lactate, now trended down and within normal limits on discharge.
.

Initially held donepizil, then later restarted.
.

.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital with right lower quadrant 
pain.  You underwent a cat scan which showed acute appendicitis. 
 You were taken to the operating room to have your appendix 
removed.  You are slowly recovering from your surgery and you 
are cleared for discharge home with the following instructions:  


You were admitted to the hospital with acute appendicitis. You 
were taken to the operating room and had your appendix removed 
laparoscopically.  You tolerated the procedure well and are now 
being discharged home with the following instructions:
Please follow up at the appointment in clinic listed below. We 
also generally recommend that patients follow up with their 
primary care provider after having surgery. We have scheduled an 
appointment for you listed below.
ACTIVITY:
 
Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
You may climb stairs. 
You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you 
see your surgeon at your next visit.
Don't lift more than ___ lbs for ___ weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
 
HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
You may feel weak or "washed out" a couple weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
You could have a poor appetite for a couple days. Food may seem 
unappealing.
All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time.  If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
Tomorrow you may shower and remove the gauzes over your 
incisions. Under these dressings you have small plastic bandages 
called steristrips. Do not remove steri-strips for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that might be on your 
incision.) But if they fall off before that that's okay.
Your incisions may be slightly red around the stitches. This is 
normal.
You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining 
your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call 
your surgeon.
You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain 
medicaitons. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as 
Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of 
magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving 
the bowels, call your surgeon. 
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness". 
Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is 
getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
You will receive a prescription for pain medicine to take by 
mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directed. Do not 
take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more 
medicine at one time than prescribed. Do not drink alcohol or 
drive while taking narcotic pain medication.
Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you cough 
or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently.
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.
 
DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your surgeon if you develop:
- worsening abdominal pain
- sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours
- temperature of 101 degrees or higher
- severe diarrhea
- vomiting
- redness around the incision that is spreading
- increased swelling around the incision
- excessive bruising around the incision
- cloudy fluid coming from the wound
- bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the 
wound
- an increase in drainage from the wound

Mrs. ___ is a ___ year old female with a history of DMII, 
depression, and PTSD who presents with 2 days of chest pain in 
the setting of 9 weeks of chronic watery, nonbloody diarrhea and 
30lb weight loss.
  
# Diarrhea - The patient had several bowel movements during her 
hospital stay, but no evidence of diarrhea.  She was negative 
for C. difficile, parasites, and other enteric pathogens.  Her 
metformin was held as this can have GI side-effects.  She was 
also given a lactose-free diet due to history of lactose 
intolerance.  She should continue to follow a lactose-free diet 
and would recommend avoiding metformin and using other agents 
for diabetes control.  If her symptoms recur, she should 
follow-up with a gastroenterologist as an outpatient.  She had a 
colonoscopy in ___ with hyperplastic polyps removed.  
  
# Chest pain - The patient was ruled out for an MI.  Her chest 
pain was vague and migratory and was felt to be unlikely cardiac 
in origin.  
  
# Weight loss - The patient has had a documented weight loss of 
30 pounds in the past month (262 -> 230).  The patient endorsed 
fear of eating as a result of her diarrhea, and her weight loss 
may be related to poor PO intake.  However, she did eat multiple 
meals without difficulty in the hospital.  Her weight should 
continue to be followed as an outpatient, and if weight loss 
continues, additional evaluation may be warranted.  
  
# Oral plaque - The patient stated that she was previously told 
she had thrush.  The plaque on her tongue was felt to not be 
thrush and to be related to poor oral hygiene.  She was 
recommended to brush her teeth and tongue and nystatin was 
stopped.
  
# PTSD/depression - The patient had a recent psychiatrist 
admission with cessation of many of her prior medications.  She 
was seen by psychiatry and social work as an inpatient.  Given 
her history of regression in psychiatric units, psychiatry 
recommended rehab placement with outpatient psychiatry 
follow-up.  She would likely benefit from referral to an 
intensive day treatment programs (she has expressed interestin 
___ on discharge from rehab.  Psychiatry also recommended 
stopping Prozac and starting Effexor 25 mg tid, and increasing 
the dose every ___ days as tolearted to 300mg a day.
  
# Diabetes mellitus, type 2 - Metformin was held given its 
well-known GI side effects, and the patient was covered with a 
QIDACHS insulin sliding scale.  On discharge, she should start 
taking glyburide 2.5 mg daily, and the dose of this medication 
should be titrated up as needed for glucose control.  Of note, 
her insulin requirement has decreased dramatically from a year 
prior when she was on NPH 30 mg QAM and 40 mg QPM.  Her HbA1c 
was 6.2%.  Her decreased insulin requirement is likely related 
to her weight loss. 
  
# Hypothyroidism - The patient is on levothyroxine 75 mg daily 
per OMR.  This medication was not on her medication list from 
her most recent OSH discharge and was restarted on this 
discharge.  
  
# Hypertension - The patient was continued on amlodipine, 
lisinopril, and atenolol.  She remained hypertensive with 
systolics up to 150-160 on this regimen and hydrochlorothiazide 
was restarted on discharge.  
  
# Tobacco abuse - patient was given a nicotine patch.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,

You were admitted to the hospital after a laparoscopic sigmoid 
colectomy for surgical management of your recurrent 
diverticulitis. You have recovered from this procedure well and 
you are now ready to return home. Samples from your colon were 
taken and this tissue has been sent to the pathology department 
for analysis. You will receive these pathology results at your 
follow-up appointment. If there is an urgent need for the 
surgeon to contact you regarding these results they will contact 
you before this time. You have tolerated a regular diet, passing 
gas and your pain is controlled with pain medications by mouth. 
You may return home to finish your recovery.

Please monitor your bowel function closely. You may or may not 
have had a bowel movement prior to your discharge which is 
acceptable, however it is important that you have a bowel 
movement in the next ___ days. After anesthesia it is not 
uncommon for patient聮s to have some decrease in bowel function 
but you should not have prolonged constipation. Some loose stool 
and passing of small amounts of dark, old appearing blood are 
expected however, if you notice that you are passing bright red 
blood with bowel movements or having loose stool without 
improvement please call the office or go to the emergency room 
if the symptoms are severe. If you are taking narcotic pain 
medications there is a risk that you will have some 
constipation. Please take an over the counter stool softener 
such as Colace, and if the symptoms does not improve call the 
office. If you have any of the following symptoms please call 
the office for advice or go to the emergency room if severe: 
increasing abdominal distension, increasing abdominal pain, 
nausea, vomiting, inability to tolerate food or liquids, 
prolonged loose stool, or constipation. 

You have ___ laparoscopic surgical incisions on your abdomen 
which are closed with internal sutures and the skin glue 
Dermabond. These are healing well however it is important that 
you monitor these areas for signs and symptoms of infection 
including: increasing redness of the incision lines, 
white/green/yellow/malodorous drainage, increased pain at the 
incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area. Please call the office if you develop any 
of these symptoms or a fever. You may go to the emergency room 
if your symptoms are severe. You may shower; pat the incisions 
dry with a towel, do not rub. The small incisions may be left 
open to the air. If closed with steri-strips, these will fall 
off over time, please do not remove them. Please no baths or 
swimming for 6 weeks after surgery unless told otherwise by Dr. 
___ Dr. ___.

You will be prescribed a small amount of the pain medication 
Oxycodone. Please take this medication exactly as prescribed. 
You may take Tylenol as recommended for pain. Please do not take 
more than 3000mg of Tylenol daily. Do not drink alcohol while 
taking narcotic pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive 
a car while taking narcotic pain medication. 

No heavy lifting greater than 6 lbs for until your first 
post-operative visit after surgery. Please no strenuous activity 
until this time unless instructed otherwise by Dr. ___ Dr. 
___.

You were also admitted to the cardiology service where we 
managed your atrial fibrillation. You were briefly on an IV 
medication that helped bring your heart rate down and eventually 
made it back to a normal rhythm. We increased your metoprolol to 
manage this.

Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care! Our hope 
is that you will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. Good luck!

Discharge Instructions:
Instructions After Orthopedic Surgery
 
- You were in the hospital for orthopedic surgery. It is normal 
to feel tired or "washed out" after surgery, and this feeling 
should improve over the first few days to week. 
- Resume your regular activities as tolerated, but please follow 
your weight bearing precautions strictly at all times.
 
Medicines
 
- Resume taking your home medications unless specifically 
instructed to stop by your surgeon. Please talk to your primary 
care doctor within the next ___ weeks regarding this 
hospitalization and any changes to your home medications that 
may be necessary.
- Do not drink alcohol, drive, or operate machinery while you 
are taking narcotic pain relievers (oxycodone/dilaudid).
- As your pain lessens, decrease the amount of narcotic pain 
relievers you are taking. Instead, take acetaminophen (also 
called tylenol). Follow all instructions on the medication 
bottle and never take more than 4,000mg of tylenol in a single 
day.
- If you need medication refills, call your surgeon's office 
3-to-4 days before you need the refill. Your prescriptions will 
be mailed to your home.
- Please take lovenox for 2 weeks to help prevent the formation 
of blood clots.
 
Constipation
 
- Both surgery and narcotic pain relievers can cause 
constipation. Please follow the advice below to help prevent 
constipation.
- Drink 8 glasses of water and/or other fluids like juice, tea, 
and broth to stay well hydrated.
- Eat foods that are high in fiber like fruits and vegetables.
- Please take a stool softener like docusate (also called 
colace) to help prevent constipation while you are taking 
narcotic pain relievers. 
- You may also take a laxative such as senna (also called 
Senokot) to help promote regular bowel movements. 
- You can buy senna or colace over the counter. Stop taking them 
if your bowel movements become loose. If your bowel movements 
continue to stay loose after stopping these medications, please 
call your doctor.
 
Incision
 
- Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
surgeon if you experience severe pain, increased swelling, 
decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, redness or 
drainage at the incision site.
- You can get the wound wet/take a shower starting 3 days after 
surgery. Let water run over the incision and do not vigorously 
scrub the surgical site. Pat the area dry after showering.
- No baths or swimming for at least 4 weeks after surgery.
- Your staples/sutures will be taken out at your 2-week follow 
up appointment. No dressing is needed if your wound is 
non-draining. 
- You may put an ice pack on your surgical site, but do not put 
the ice pack directly on your skin (place a towel between your 
skin and the ice pack), and do not leave it in place for more 
than 20 minutes at a time.
 
Activity
 - Your weight-bearing restrictions are: weight bearing as 
tolerated in the left lower extremity.

WBAT BLE. ROMAT. No limitations - just use walker

- then can leave open to air

Surgery Discharge Instructions
Brain Hemorrhage with Surgery 
路 You underwent a surgery called a craniotomy to have blood 
removed from your brain. 
路 It is best to keep your incision open to air but it is ok to 
cover it when outside. 
路 Call your surgeon if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage.  

Activity
路     We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, 
climbing, or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up 
appointment.
路     You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.
路     No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating 
medication.
路     If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 
路     No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months.  

Medications
路     Please do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 
路     *** You have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam). 
This medication helps to prevent seizures. Please continue this 
medication as indicated on your discharge instruction. It is 
important that you take this medication consistently and on 
time. 
路     You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort 
if you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

What You ___ Experience:
路     You may have difficulty paying attention, concentrating, 
and remembering new information.
路     Emotional and/or behavioral difficulties are common. 
路     Feeling more tired, restlessness, irritability, and mood 
swings are also common.
路     You may also experience some post-operative swelling 
around your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 
路     You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal 
from the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 
路     Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids 
and eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics 
(prescription pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool 
softener.

Headaches:
路     Headache is one of the most common symptoms after a brain 
bleed. 
路     Most headaches are not dangerous but you should call your 
doctor if the headache gets worse, develop arm or leg weakness, 
increased sleepiness, and/or have nausea or vomiting with a 
headache. 
路     Mild pain medications may be helpful with these headaches 
but avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless 
prescribed by your doctor. 
路     There are other things that can be done to help with your 
headaches: avoid caffeine, get enough sleep, daily exercise, 
relaxation/ meditation, massage, acupuncture, heat or ice packs.

fluid from your body by urinating. You did well on this 
medication, and the lower extremity swelling in your legs 
decreased. Furthermore, you had less shortness of breath while 
you were ambulating.

aspirin you had been given was stopped on this admission, and

episodes of bleeding in your urine or stool during this 
admission. You will require close followup with a cardiologist, 
and appointments have been arranged on your behalf below.

and oral antibiotics. You tolerated this therapy well, and you 
had no evidence of bacteria in your urine on repeat urine 
cultures. You completed your antibiotic regimen prior to leaving 
the hospital.

Please followup at the appointments that have been arranged on 
your behalf below.

stay. We wish you the best of luck!

Instructions After Surgery
You were in the hospital for surgery.  It is normal to feel 
tired or washed out after surgery. Rest is important and will 
help you feel better. Walking is also important. It will help 
prevent problems and help you feel stronger and have more 
energy.  Just remember to pace yourself and rest when you feel 
tired.
Follow these important instructions below:
- Keep your dressing in place for 48 hours after surgery. This 
may be removed on ___.
What medications do I need to take?
-Take your pain medicine the way your doctor tells you to. 
o Do not drink alcohol, drive, or use machinery while you are 
taking narcotic pain medicine.
o As your pain lessens, decrease the amount of pain medicine you 

are taking.
-Narcotic pain medicine can cause constipation. To make it 
easier to have a bowel movement:
o Drink extra water and other fluids like juice, tea, and broth.
o Take a stool softener like docusate.  Docusate is also called 
Colace. You can buy it over the counter.
-Resume all of your other home medications unless advised 
otherwise.
What activities am I allowed at home?
-Walking is encouraged.  
-Do not do any strenuous exercise like aerobics, running or 
weight lifting until your surgeon says it is ok.
-Do not lift more than 10 pounds for ___ weeks. Ten pounds is as 

heavy as a gallon of milk. 
How do I care for my incisions?
-It is ok to leave your incisions uncovered.  You may also cover 

them with dry gauze if your incisions are draining or to protect 

them from irritation.
-Do not shower or get wound wet for 48 hours after drain is 
removed. After this you may shower normally.  Do not scrub your 
incision.  Gently wash with soapy water and pat wound dry 
afterwards.  Please do not immerse wound in bath, swimming, or 
sauna for ___ weeks or until wound completely healed/closed.

Follow up information
-Please go to your scheduled follow up appointment with your 
surgeon.
-Please follow up with your primary care physician ___ ___ 
weeks for a post-operative follow up.
-Call your surgeon if you have any questions.  Call the clinic 
during regular business hours.  If after hours or on the 
weekend, call the hospital operator at ___ and ask to 
page the ENT resident on call.

# Inferior STEMI: Patient presented initially with chest pain 
and was found to have elevated troponins. Serial EKGs showed 
progressing q-waves in the inferior leads as well. While in 
hospital, he continued to have chest pain and on ___, he under 
cardiac catheterization which demonstrated a complete occlusion 
of the proximal RCA. This was treated with a DES and thought to 
be the culprit lesion. In addition he had 50% stenosis of the 
LAD and 80% stenosis of the OM2 and DM. LVED was 16 mm Hg, 
consistent with mild acute (on likely chronic) LV diastolic 
dysfunction. His CK peaked at 2335 with MB peaking at 210. He 
had mild ST elevation in the inferior leads. Echocardiogram 
documented an LVEF of 40-45% with severe hypokinesis to akinesis 
of the inferior wall, basal to mid inferoseptum and basal 
inferolateral walls. The RV was noted to be mildly dilated and 
with mild dysfunction. The patient was started on metoprolol 
with dose titration and atorvastatin 80 mg. His lisinopril was 
decreased to 5 mg to allow for increased beta-blockade. He was 
started on clopidgrel which he should receive for one year. His 
aspirin should be continued for life; he had been on this 
medication prior to admission. He was repeatedly counseled on 
the importance of these medications and prior to discharge, 
extensive review of his medications were discussed. Patient was 
able to work with physical therapy prior to discharge with no 
concerns or impedance to discharge. He was provided with 
referral information to outpatient cardiac rehabilitation. He 
was set up with close follow up with his PCP and his new 
cardiologist.

# Hypertension: Per patient, his blood pressure had been well 
controlled prior to admission. During his hospitalization, he 
was started on metoprolol for post-MI secondary prevention and 
for his residual CAD. Because of this, his hydrocholorothiazide 
was discontinued. His lisinopril was decreased on discharge to 5 
mg daily to allow for increased beta-blockade effect. He did 
have occasional blood pressure readings in the ___, but was 
asymptomatic during these periods. He worked with physical 
therapy prior to discharge, and there was no concern for 
orthostatic symptoms, so he was discharged home. He should be 
reassessed in outpatient follow-up regarding titrating his ACE 
inhibitor as tolerated.

# NAFLD: Patient has documented history of non-alcoholic fatty 
liver disease noted by his PCP. He was started on atorvastatin 
during his hospitalization for secondary prevention post-MI. He 
was discharged with recommendation to follow up with his PCP, 
with specific mention to his PCP that he should be monitored for 
liver toxicity.

and his wife were counseled multiple times on the benefits and 
importance of quitting smoking. In particular, the association 
between his presentation and cardiac disease were stressed.

but you should not have prolonged constipation. Some loose stool 
and passing of small amounts of dark, old appearing blood are 
expected. However, if you notice that you are passing bright red 
blood with bowel movements or having loose stool without 
improvement please call the office or go to the emergency room 
if the symptoms are severe. If you are taking narcotic pain 
medications there is a risk that you will have some

such as Colace, and if the symptoms do not improve call the 
office. If you have any of the following symptoms please call 
the office for advice or go to the emergency room if severe: 
increasing abdominal distension, increasing abdominal pain, 
nausea, vomiting, inability to tolerate food or liquids, 
prolonged loose stool, or extended constipation.  
 
You have ___ laparoscopic surgical incisions on your abdomen 
which are closed with internal sutures and a skin glue called 
Dermabond. These are healing well however it is important that 
you monitor these areas for signs and symptoms of infection 
including: increasing redness of the incision lines, 
white/green/yellow/malodorous drainage, increased pain at the 
incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area. Please call the office if you develop any 
of these symptoms or a fever. You may go to the emergency room 
if your symptoms are severe.

them. Please no baths or swimming for 6 weeks after surgery 
unless told otherwise by your surgical team.

pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive a car while 
taking narcotic pain medication.

Personal Care:
1. You may keep your bilateral breast dressings in place until

skin, with soap and water.

times per day.
4. A written record of the daily output from each drain should 
be brought to every follow-up appointment. your drains will be 
removed as soon as possible when the daily output tapers off to 
an acceptable amount.
5. DO NOT wear a bra for 3 weeks. You may wear a camisole for 
comfort as desired.  You may also wear a front zip up or front 
closing sports bra that is not too tight. No underwires.
6. You may shower daily with assistance as needed.  Be sure to 
secure your drains to a laniard that hangs down from your neck 
so they don't hang down and pull out.
7. The Dermabond skin glue will begin to flake off in about ___ 
days.
8. No pressure on your chest or abdomen
9. Okay to shower, but no baths until after directed by Dr. 
___.
.
Activity:
1. You may resume your regular diet. 
2. Keep hips flexed at all times, and then gradually stand 
upright as tolerated.
3. DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity for 6 weeks following surgery.
.
Medications:
1. Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise 
and take any new meds as ordered .
2. You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain.  You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength 
Tylenol for mild pain as directed on the packaging.  Please note 
that Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient 
so do not take these meds with additional Tylenol.
3. Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol.
4. Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking 
the prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softerner if you wish.
5. Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber.
.
Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the following:
1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s).
2. A large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s).
3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication.
.
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications. 
* If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness,swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you.
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you.
.

You are being discharged with drains in place. Drain care is a 
clean procedure. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm 
water before performing drain care. Perform drainage care twice 
a day. Try to empty the drain at the same time each day. Pull 
the stopper out of the drainage bottle and empty the drainage 
fluid into the measuring cup. Record the amount of drainage 
fluid on the record sheet. Reestablish drain suction.

Discharge Instructions:
Post Surgery Wound Care 

 

Overview

  

Your doctor has placed sutures (stitches) to keep the incision 
closed for proper wound healing. Sometimes, sutures need to be 
removed in a few weeks. Sometimes, the sutures are all under the 
skin and will eventual dissolve on their own and do not need to 
be removed. 

 

In either case, please follow these routine wound care 
instructions.

 

Leave the original bandage that was applied at the time of your 
surgery in place for 48 hours. If the bandage should become 
loose, reinforce the dressing with surgical tape.

 

After approximately 48 hours, you can gently remove the bandage.

 

If you have steri-strips on your incision (little white paper 
tapes), keep them in place until they begin to fall off on their 
own. Do not pull the steri-strips off as this could put stress 
on the incision line. When the steri-strips start to peel off, 
they can be gently washed off.

 

Please try to keep the incision line clean and dry. You can 
shower and gently wash the incision line with soap and water. 
Dry the incision area and keep the incision line open to air.

 

It is not necessary to apply antibiotic ointment, alcohol, 
hydrogen peroxide, or a new bandage to the incision line. If 
your sutures get caught on your clothing or there is a small 
amount of drainage from the incision, you may want to cover it 
with small gauze for your own comfort. If so, please use as 
little tape as possible to hold the gauze in place as tape can 
irritate the skin.

 

A small amount of drainage from the incision in the first few 
days after surgery is not unusual and it will probably resolve 
on its own. However, if you should notice bleeding from the 
surgical site, apply firm direct pressure for ten minutes. If 
the bleeding persists, reapply firm direct pressure for an 
additional ten minutes. If the bleeding does not stop after 20 
minutes, call our contact phone numbers or go to the nearest 
emergency room for assistance.

 

What to Avoid  

 

Please avoid the following:

 

Do not submerge the incision line under water for a prolonged 
period of time with activities like taking a bath, swimming, or 
sitting in a hot tub.

 

Do not participate in any vigorous activities or exercises that 
may put stress on the incision. 

 

Do not take aspirin, ibuprofen, or any other nonsteroidal 
anti-inflammatory medication that may cause problems with 
bleeding unless instructed by your doctor.

 

Do not apply perfumes or scented lotions to the sutures as this 
may cause irritation.

 

When to Call the Doctor

  

Please contact us immediately if you develop:

 

Fevers, chills, or night sweats

 

Increasing redness, pain, or pus at the incision

 

Bleeding that does not stop with firm pressure

 

Followup Care 

 

If your sutures need to be removed, this is usually done ___ 
weeks after surgery. Even if your sutures will dissolve, the 
doctor usually likes to examine the incision while it is 
healing. Therefore, you should have been scheduled for a 
follow-up appointment in clinic at the time of your discharge 
from surgery. As this appointment is very important, please 
contact the clinic if you do not have one scheduled or you need 
to change the date and/or time.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. ___ was admitted to the neurology ICU for monitoring 
following a seizure and new findings of multiple intracranial 
hemorrhages on non-contrast head CT. After close monitoring, she 
was transferred to the neurology floor for further care.

#NEUROLOGY
She was placed on telemetry monitoring with seizure precautions, 
blood pressure was kept under 160 with her home blood pressure 
medications. She was contined on Keppra 1g BID, and she had no 
further seizures. This was decreased to 750mg BID and the 
patient remained clinically seizure-free. Extended routine EEG 
was normal. 

Repeat NCHCT showed stable hemorrhages. CTA was unremarkable. 
MRI unable to be performed due to pacemaker. Differential for 
her bleeds included amyloid angiopathy, metastatic disease, or 
emboli which could potentially be clot vs septic. For further 
workup, TTE was performed which was unchanged from prior with no 
specific embolic source such as a thrombus or vegetation found, 
and CT torso with and without contrast done which showed 
potentially suspicious cystic lesions in the kidney. For further 
workup, renal ultrasound was ordered. This showed that these 
lesions were most likely benign. Potential serum tumor markers 
were evaluated and were also normal. The leading diagnosis for 
the etiology of the brain hemorrhages is therefore amyloid 
angiopathy.

#CARDIOLOGY - she was continued on all of her home medications. 
TTE as above. Telemetry monitoring with atrial fibrillation. 
Warfarin was stopped and the patient and her family were 
counseled that given the high risk for recurrent brain 
hemorrhage, this should not be restarted in the future. She 
should start aspirin 81mg daily 1 week after the incident, which 
is ___. 

#ID - infectious workup, including CBC, UA, and CXR were 
negative. TTE with no evidence of endocarditis. She remained 
afebrile during her stay with no signs or symptoms of infection.

#Physical therapy saw the patient during her stay and on day of 
discharge recommended home with home ___. She will be going home 
with her daughter, who assures us that she will also have 24 
hour supervision.

instability for which she has planned neurosurgery in ___. On 
admission exam her mental status was at baseline but she did 
appear to have new development of postural instability and an 
increase in involuntary movements over the past few months

symptoms. All her other labs were within normal limits during

in setting of infection given pyuria (see below). Her 
instability improved substantially during her hospitalization.
