Discharge Instructions:
Please follow the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and 
uneventful recovery.
 
ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs.
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit.
- Don't lift more than 10 lbs for 6 weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
- You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
- You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
- You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
- All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
- Your incision may be slightly red around the edges. This is 
normal.
- If you have steri strips, do not remove them for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that are on your incision.) But 
if they fall off before that that's okay).
- You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
- It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This 
will go away.
- Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
- Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
- You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
- You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
- Over the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluitds and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
-You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine 
as directed.
- Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take 
more medicine at one time than prescribed.
- Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
- Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
- If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
- Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon.

the aortic valve. BAV x 2 was performed without the need for

Brief Hospital Course:
___ y/o F with hx of chronic postprandial pain presenting with 
likely acute pancreatitis.  
 
#Acute Pancreatitis/RUQ pain: In the setting of chronic 
postprandial RUQ pain, elevated lipase to 395, history of acute 
n/v, and radiologic findings (parapancreatic fat stranding, 
pancretic calcifications), acute pancreatitis was thought to be 
the most likely diagnosis. She received 3L of fluid in the ED, 
and was normotensive/non-tachycardic on the floor, producing 
adequate urine output. Given the history of chronic postprandial 
RUQ pain, gallstone pancreatitis was thought to be the most 
likely underlying etiology, with the stone having "passed" by 
the time of RUQ U/S. However, AP and Tbili were not elevated (86 
and 0.2, respectively), and the complete lack of findings (e.g. 
biliary sludge, mild CBD dilatation) on RUQ is atypical for 
gallstone pancreatitis. Possible other etiologies included 
drug-induced causes (namenda, donepezil, and celecoxib all have 
very rare (<1%) associations with pancreatits). Finally, her CT 
abd/pelvis was consistent with acute on chronic pancreatitis, 
and several cysts were visualized (though it was noted to be a 
suboptimal study for pancreatic visualization). Irritation from 
cystic material may have also been involved in her presentation. 
In any case, her ___ criteria score on admission was ___, 
consistent with mild pancreatitis, with an extremely low 
mortality (<5%). By the morning following admission, her lipase 
had dropped to 29 suggesting resolution of her pancreatitis and 
she was able to tolerate po intake. She will follow-up with GI 
as an outpatient for further evaluation of her pancreatic cysts 
with likely endoscopic ultrasound. 

#LLQ pain: LLQ pain was concerning for diverticulitis vs 
constipation. Notably, she does not complain of pain at rest, 
which is surpising in the context of diverticulitis. Her CT did 
not show any signs of diverticulitis or inflammation in LLQ. 
Further, she did have a leukocytosis and was afebrile, and has 
symptomatically improved on NPO and fluids alone--this is not 
typical of diverticulitis. Given that thse has not had a BM in 3 
days, it is thus likely that this pain is related to 
constipation. Her pain improved after bowel movement. Patient 
takes narcotics as an outpatient and she will be placed on 
laxative regimen. 

#Right leg swelling, calf pain: Given the history of an 
athroscopic R knee procedure in ___, it is possible 
that the R leg swelling compared with left leg swelling is her 
baseline. However, given her history of left ankle sprain (and 
thus possibility of impaired mobility for the past month) and 
her complaint of right calf pain, there was a possibility ___ 
DVT, but ___ US was negative. 

#L ankle pain: Patient complained of point tenderness over the 
left lateral ankle. She had a fall in 2 months ago and an ankle 
film one month ago that did not show any fracture. As she has 
point tenderness, another x-ray was performed. It showed 
sclerosis at the distal tibia but no abnormalities at the site 
of her pain. She was also able to tolerate standing without pain 
and no further imaging was needed.

#CHRONIC PANNUS ULCERATIONS 
#NUTRITION
Patient with chronic MRSA-infected ulcerations of the 
intertriginous zone of her pannus requiring multiple 
hospitalizations. Plastics has been following as outpatient with 
for consideration of panniculectomy after medical clearance. 
Wound care nurse consulted. Wounds were redressed Qd. Plastic

evaluation for pre-operative assessment. Per nutrition, patient 
continued on multivitamin with minerals, and continued on 10 day 
course of ascorbic acid ___ daily (___) and 10 day course 
of vitamin A ___ units (___). Zinc level was sent, normal. 
Dermatology thought the most likely etiology of her ulcers was 
her arterial disease, less likely infectious or pyoderma 
gangrenosum. Cardiology evaluated patient to perform 
pre-operative risk stratification, was considering intervention 
given her history of CAD with Lcirc CTO, but decided against 
this given small affected territory and presence of collaterals. 
She was deemed to have at least 3 risk factors, corresponding to 
a predicted risk of ~9% for major cardiac complications, 
recommended continuing aspirin but from cardiac standpoint does 
not need DAPT. Uncomplicated Panniculectomy was completed on 
___. Wound vac and 2 JP drains were left in place on discharge 
with plan for followup in Plastic Surgery clinic on ___ for 
removal.

3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.

follow-up appointment in three (3) weeks.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. You may wrap the knee with an ace bandage 
for added compression. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc).

weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking this medication.

dry. REMOVE one week after surgery. It is okay to shower five 
days after surgery but no tub baths, swimming, or submerging 
your incision until after your four (4) week checkup. You may 
place a dry sterile dressing on the wound after the Silverlon 
dressing is removed, otherwise leave it open to air. Check wound 
regularly for signs of infection such as redness or thick yellow 
drainage. Staples will be removed by at your follow-up visit in 
3 weeks.

instructed, and wound checks.

Discharge Instructions
Brain Hemorrhage with Surgery

Brain Surgery

路 You underwent a surgery called a craniectomy. A portion of 
your skull was removed to allow your brain to swell. You then 
underwent cranioplasty, which replaced the bone. You no longer 
need to wear a helmet when out of bed. 

It is best to keep your incision open to air but it is ok to 
cover it when outside. 

路 Call your ___ if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Spine Surgery - Spinal Fusion

路 Your incision is closed with staples . You will need staple 
removal. 

路 Do not apply any lotions or creams to the site. 

路 Please keep your incision dry until removal of your staple

路 Please avoid swimming for two weeks after staple removal.

路 Call your ___ if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Activity

路 You do not need any bracing or helmets at this time. 

路 We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.

路 You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.

路 No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 

路 If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 

路 No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

路 Do  NOT smoke. Smoking can affect your healing and fusion.

Medications

路 Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc聟 until cleared by your 
neurosurgeon. This can interfere with your spine fusion.

路 You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

 
What You ___ Experience:

路 You may have difficulty paying attention, concentrating, and 
remembering new information.

路 Emotional and/or behavioral difficulties are common. 

路 Feeling more tired, restlessness, irritability, and mood 
swings are also common.

路 You may also experience some post-operative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 

路 You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 

路 Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics (prescription 
pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool softener.

Headaches:

路 Headache is one of the most common symptoms after a brain 
bleed. 

路 Most headaches are not dangerous but you should call your 
doctor if the headache gets worse, develop arm or leg weakness, 
increased sleepiness, and/or have nausea or vomiting with a 
headache. 

路 Mild pain medications may be helpful with these headaches but 
avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless prescribed 
by your doctor. 

路 There are other things that can be done to help with your 
headaches: avoid caffeine, get enough sleep, daily exercise, 
relaxation/ meditation, massage, acupuncture, heat or ice packs.

Discharge Instructions
Brain Hemorrhage with Surgery

Surgery
聲You underwent a surgery called a craniotomy to have blood 
removed from your brain. 
聲Please keep your sutures and staple along your incision dry 
until they are removed.
聲It is best to keep your incision open to air but it is ok to 
cover it when outside. 
聲Call your surgeon if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Activity
聲We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.
聲You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.
聲No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 
聲If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 
聲No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

Medications
聲Please do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 
聲You have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam) for 6 
additional days. This medication helps to prevent seizures. 
Please continue this medication as indicated on your discharge 
instruction. It is important that you take this medication 
consistently and on time. 
聲You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

What You ___ Experience:
聲You may have difficulty paying attention, concentrating, and 
remembering new information.
聲Emotional and/or behavioral difficulties are common. 
聲Feeling more tired, restlessness, irritability, and mood 
swings are also common.
聲You may also experience some post-operative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 
聲You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 
聲Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics (prescription 
pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool softener.

Headaches:
聲Headache is one of the most common symptoms after a brain 
bleed. 
聲Most headaches are not dangerous but you should call your 
doctor if the headache gets worse, develop arm or leg weakness, 
increased sleepiness, and/or have nausea or vomiting with a 
headache. 
聲Mild pain medications may be helpful with these headaches but 
avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless prescribed 
by your doctor. 
聲There are other things that can be done to help with your 
headaches: avoid caffeine, get enough sleep, daily exercise, 
relaxation/ meditation, massage, acupuncture, heat or ice packs. 


When to Call Your Doctor at ___ for:

site.

Call ___ and go to the nearest Emergency Room if you experience

聲Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, or loss of balance or 
coordination
聲Sudden severe headaches with no known reason

Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M admitted to the Acute Care Trauma 
Surgery Service on ___ after a mechanical fall on Coumadin 
for afib. CT scan showed a left gluteal hematoma with active 
extravasation. His INR was 4.0 on admission and he subsequently 
received 10 mg IV Vitamin K, K-centra, and 1 unit of fresh 
frozen plasma. Interventional radiology was consulted and 
deferred intervention at that time. He was admitted to the floor 
for serial hematocrits and hemodynamic monitoring.

On HD2 his hematocrit continued to trend down and he was 
transfused with 2 units of packed red blood cells. On HD3 his 
hematocrit continued to trend down and he received 2 more units 
of packed red blood cells. Interventional radiology was 
reconsulted and preformed a successful Gel-Foam embolization of 
the posterior division of the left internal iliac artery. His 
hematocrit levels remained stable.

___ Diabetic Center was consulted for high glucose in the 
setting of elevated creatinine and recommended transitioning to 
Lantus, glipizide, and Januvia with further outpatient 
monitoring of his creatinine and glucose. 

On HD7 he had an episode of weakness, lightheadedness, and 
hypotension to the 60's systolic. His hematocrit was stable, 
troponins were negative for elevation, his ECG was baseline 
atrial fibrillation. He received 1 Liter of Lactated Ringers and 
his symptoms improved and was hemodynamically stable. He 
received 1 unit of packed red blood cells for suspected 
decreased oxygen carrying capacity in setting of anemia with 
history of heart failure. On HD8, he was initially feeling well 
but had an episode of weakness/dizziness improved with sitting 
and laying down. The MERIT team was consulted and recommended 
decreasing his lisinopril dose. He had subsequent improvement of 
symptoms. 

The remainder of his hospital course can be summarized below:
Neuro: The patient was alert and oriented throughout 
hospitalization; pain was managed with oral acetaminophen. 
CV: The patient had symptomatic hypotension resolved after 
intervention as described above; vital signs were routinely 
monitored.
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.

without difficulty.

infection, of which there were none.
HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs 
of bleeding and he was transfused with red blood cells as 
needed.

dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get 
up and ambulate as early as possible. His home coumdain dosing 
was restarted on HD7 and INR levels were monitored.

and hemodynamically stable.  The patient was tolerating a diet, 
ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. He was discharged to his sister's house with ___ 
services to assist with wound care and INR monitoring.  The 
patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions 
with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge 
plan. Follow up appointments were scheduled.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. ___ was admitted to the neurology ICU for monitoring 
following a seizure and new findings of multiple intracranial 
hemorrhages on non-contrast head CT. After close monitoring, she 
was transferred to the neurology floor for further care.

#NEUROLOGY
She was placed on telemetry monitoring with seizure precautions, 
blood pressure was kept under 160 with her home blood pressure 
medications. She was contined on Keppra 1g BID, and she had no 
further seizures. This was decreased to 750mg BID and the 
patient remained clinically seizure-free. Extended routine EEG 
was normal. 

Repeat NCHCT showed stable hemorrhages. CTA was unremarkable. 
MRI unable to be performed due to pacemaker. Differential for 
her bleeds included amyloid angiopathy, metastatic disease, or 
emboli which could potentially be clot vs septic. For further 
workup, TTE was performed which was unchanged from prior with no 
specific embolic source such as a thrombus or vegetation found, 
and CT torso with and without contrast done which showed 
potentially suspicious cystic lesions in the kidney. For further 
workup, renal ultrasound was ordered. This showed that these 
lesions were most likely benign. Potential serum tumor markers 
were evaluated and were also normal. The leading diagnosis for 
the etiology of the brain hemorrhages is therefore amyloid 
angiopathy.

#CARDIOLOGY - she was continued on all of her home medications. 
TTE as above. Telemetry monitoring with atrial fibrillation. 
Warfarin was stopped and the patient and her family were 
counseled that given the high risk for recurrent brain 
hemorrhage, this should not be restarted in the future. She 
should start aspirin 81mg daily 1 week after the incident, which 
is ___. 

#ID - infectious workup, including CBC, UA, and CXR were 
negative. TTE with no evidence of endocarditis. She remained 
afebrile during her stay with no signs or symptoms of infection.

#Physical therapy saw the patient during her stay and on day of 
discharge recommended home with home ___. She will be going home 
with her daughter, who assures us that she will also have 24 
hour supervision.

#Falls & Postural Instability: Patient presented with multiple 
recent falls and increasing sensation of poor balance. Of note 
one prior fall resulted in C2 laminar fracture with resulting 
instability for which she has planned neurosurgery in ___. On 
admission exam her mental status was at baseline but she did 
appear to have new development of postural instability and an 
increase in involuntary movements over the past few months 
leading up to her most recent fall on the night prior to 
admission. A CT head done in the ED was unremarkable, and 
subsequent head MRI and spinal cord CT showed no evidence of a 
new acute ischemic or hemorrhagic processes. Neurology was 
consulted, who suggested the symptoms could be consistent with 
high lamotrigine and/or gabapentin levels, so we decreased her 
lamotrigine dosage from 400mg oral DAILY to 300mg oral daily. 
Lamotrigine and gabapentin levels are pending.  ECG done on 
admission to the floor showed no evidence of new cardiac 
process. B12 was 585 and TSH was 0.81, which ruled out B12 
deficiency or hypo/hyperthyroidism as contributing to her 
symptoms. All her other labs were within normal limits during 
her time here. It is possible she may also have been orthostatic 
in setting of infection given pyuria (see below). Her 
instability improved substantially during her hospitalization. 
She worked with physical therapy and was ambulating well with a 
walker on day of discharge.

#Pyuria: Patient with asymptomatic pyuria (WBC 57) on 
presentation. Given her recent falls and instability on 
presentation we were initially also concerned for an infectious 
process. She was started on 1 dose of Cipro in ED and switched

days of treatment with the ceftriaxone. Urine cultures came back 
with contamination.

Discharge Instructions:
Please follow the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and 
uneventful recovery.
 
ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs.
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit.
- Don't lift more than 10 lbs for 6 weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
- You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
- You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
- You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
- All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
- Your incision may be slightly red around the edges. This is 
normal.
- If you have steri strips, do not remove them for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that are on your incision.) But 
if they fall off before that that's okay).
- You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
- It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This 
will go away.
- Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
- Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
- You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
- You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
- Over the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluitds and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
-You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine 
as directed.
- Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take 
more medicine at one time than prescribed.
- Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
- Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
- If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
- Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon.

demonstrated mild 3 vessel CAD without critical obstructive 
lesions. We therefore decided to perform BAV in an attempt to

fashion using a ___ JR4 catheter and a straight wire. We then 
exchanged for a ___ dual lumen pigtail catheter and LHC was 
performed. Next we exchanged for a .035 Confida wire and used 
this to advance a 22 mm x 4.5 cm True balloon retrograde across 
the aortic valve. BAV x 2 was performed without the need for 
ventricular pacing. Following this, we removed the True balloon 
and LHC was again performed using the dual lumen pigtail 
catheter. However, the patient's critical hemodynamic status 
failed to improve.

___ was an ___ yo man with H/O left ventricular 
systolic heart failure (LVEF 23%), ESRD on HD MWF via left lower 
forearm AV fistula, and severe aortic stenosis (peak/mean 
gradient 55/31, ___ 0.9) who presented with worsening shortness

stenosis. He was medically managed with ASA, statin, metoprolol,

concern that the patient might not be able to tolerate the 
procedures leading to TAVR or benefit from TAVR due to advanced 
dementia, limited functional capacity, and other comorbidities 
including ESRD. Geriatrics and palliative care were consulted. 
Extensive family meetings were held to discuss conservative vs. 
interventional treatment options. Family was agreeable to 
proceeding with palliative aortic valve balloon valvuloplasty 
with the hope that he may have some improvement in 
breathing/quality of life and assess candidacy for TAVR later. 
Unfortunately, during his cardiac catheterization with coronary 
angiography prior to planned valvuloplasty on ___, the patient 
became progressively hypotensive requiring escalating doses of 
vasopressor medications, intra-aortic balloon pump, and 
ultimately Impella device despite aortic valvuloplasty and only 
mild CAD found. The patient continued to deteriorate, mainly due 
to refractory RV failure, with progressive hypotension. After 
consultation with the patient's family (wife and daughter), it 
was felt that no further interventions were indicated or 
desired, with a focus on comfort measures. The patient expired 
at 14:58.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ y/o F with hx of chronic postprandial pain presenting with 
likely acute pancreatitis.  
 
#Acute Pancreatitis/RUQ pain: In the setting of chronic 
postprandial RUQ pain, elevated lipase to 395, history of acute 
n/v, and radiologic findings (parapancreatic fat stranding, 
pancretic calcifications), acute pancreatitis was thought to be 
the most likely diagnosis. She received 3L of fluid in the ED, 
and was normotensive/non-tachycardic on the floor, producing 
adequate urine output. Given the history of chronic postprandial 
RUQ pain, gallstone pancreatitis was thought to be the most 
likely underlying etiology, with the stone having "passed" by 
the time of RUQ U/S. However, AP and Tbili were not elevated (86 
and 0.2, respectively), and the complete lack of findings (e.g. 
biliary sludge, mild CBD dilatation) on RUQ is atypical for 
gallstone pancreatitis. Possible other etiologies included 
drug-induced causes (namenda, donepezil, and celecoxib all have 
very rare (<1%) associations with pancreatits). Finally, her CT 
abd/pelvis was consistent with acute on chronic pancreatitis, 
and several cysts were visualized (though it was noted to be a 
suboptimal study for pancreatic visualization). Irritation from 
cystic material may have also been involved in her presentation. 
In any case, her ___ criteria score on admission was ___, 
consistent with mild pancreatitis, with an extremely low 
mortality (<5%). By the morning following admission, her lipase 
had dropped to 29 suggesting resolution of her pancreatitis and 
she was able to tolerate po intake. She will follow-up with GI 
as an outpatient for further evaluation of her pancreatic cysts 
with likely endoscopic ultrasound. 

#LLQ pain: LLQ pain was concerning for diverticulitis vs 
constipation. Notably, she does not complain of pain at rest, 
which is surpising in the context of diverticulitis. Her CT did 
not show any signs of diverticulitis or inflammation in LLQ. 
Further, she did have a leukocytosis and was afebrile, and has 
symptomatically improved on NPO and fluids alone--this is not 
typical of diverticulitis. Given that thse has not had a BM in 3 
days, it is thus likely that this pain is related to 
constipation. Her pain improved after bowel movement. Patient 
takes narcotics as an outpatient and she will be placed on 
laxative regimen. 

#Right leg swelling, calf pain: Given the history of an 
athroscopic R knee procedure in ___, it is possible 
that the R leg swelling compared with left leg swelling is her 
baseline. However, given her history of left ankle sprain (and 
thus possibility of impaired mobility for the past month) and 
her complaint of right calf pain, there was a possibility ___ 
DVT, but ___ US was negative. 

#L ankle pain: Patient complained of point tenderness over the 
left lateral ankle. She had a fall in 2 months ago and an ankle 
film one month ago that did not show any fracture. As she has 
point tenderness, another x-ray was performed. It showed 
sclerosis at the distal tibia but no abnormalities at the site 
of her pain. She was also able to tolerate standing without pain 
and no further imaging was needed. 

#Hyponatremia: She was admitted with Na of 127 and it normalized 
to 136. Her urine sodium was 185 and her UOsm was 547. Her 
calculated serum osmolarity, on the other hand, was 276.2. If 
she were intravascularly depleted, this could be considered 
appropriate. Her sodium status improved after volume repletion.

#HTN: She was continued on atenolol 25 mg po BID (which was from 
the original med list). On discharge, her daughter told us she 
told us she took atenolol 25 mg po daily and diovan 80 mg po 
daily and she was restarted on this regimen at discharge. 
  
#FEN:  She was initially made NPO and given iv fluids and then 
her diet was advanced clears and then low residue diet which she 
tolerated with mild nausea.

#CHRONIC PANNUS ULCERATIONS 
#NUTRITION
Patient with chronic MRSA-infected ulcerations of the

given her history of CAD with Lcirc CTO, but decided against

night, thought to either be atelectasis or undiagnosed OSA.

for anticoagulation for DVT). Heparin gtt (___), followed

# Takotsubo stress cardiomyopathy 
Acute elevation of troponin peaking at 0.8 with concomitant TWI 
in anterolateral leads. Echo consistent with Takatsubo's likely 
from stress of asthma exacerbation. Management was targeted 
towards reducing cardiac demand by
putting her on mechanical ventilation, metoprolol, and losartan.

ASA or increasing statin given low suspicion for ACS.

(5) days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at 
least four (4) weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues 
to be non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be 
removed will be taken out at first follow up appointment two 
weeks after your surgery.

follow-up appointment in two (2) weeks.

medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).

help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  You may 
continue to take 81mg of aspirin while on this medication.  ___ 
STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.

10. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed AT FIRST POST OP 
APPOINTMENT in two (2) weeks.

11. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks.

12. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Mobilize. CPM/ROM as tolerated. Knee immobilizer x 6 
hours/day.  No strenuous exercise or heavy lifting until follow 
up appointment.

not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment in three (3) weeks.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. You may wrap the knee with an ace bandage 
for added compression. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc).

weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking this medication.

days after surgery but no tub baths, swimming, or submerging

place a dry sterile dressing on the wound after the Silverlon 
dressing is removed, otherwise leave it open to air. Check wound 
regularly for signs of infection such as redness or thick yellow 
drainage. Staples will be removed by at your follow-up visit in

ex-wife and step daughter, were able to contact patient's father 
and brother.  After discussion of clinical course and prognosis, 
decision was made to withdraw care and make comfort measures.
